text: This is a video to introduce a robotic low ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery with real-time identification of the vascular system for rectal cancer using the firefly technique. A fifty-two-year-old man was referred to our department and colonoscopy revealed an all-serophungating mass seven centimeters from the anal verge.
Our technique for robotic total mesorectal excision was the single docking dual phase technique.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the sacral promontory below the aortic bifurcation, performing a medial to lateral dissection of the sigmoid and descending colon along the avascular plane while preserving autonomic nerves.

Under white light, the anatomical blood supply around the pancreas and inferior mesenteric vein is assessed. Following the injection of 5mg ICG dye, near-infrared fluorescence imaging reveals the collateral artery near the inferior mesenteric vein and pancreas, with the collateral arteries and inferior mesenteric vein clearly visualized by the excited fluorescence.

Within five seconds of the ICG injection, the low border of the pancreas, followed by the collateral artery and the inferior mesenteric vein, are visualized in consecutive order using near-infrared fluorescence imaging.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm with a double fenestrated grasper to lift the inferior mesenteric vein, incising the peritoneum beneath it to develop the retroperitoneal space. Under near-infrared fluorescence imaging, the optimal point for inferior mesenteric vein ligation is identified, and the vein is ligated under white light with precise skeletonization.

The surgeon dissects lymph nodes around the root of the inferior mesenteric artery. ICG is injected for near-infrared fluorescence imaging to identify the left colic artery at the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery. The surgeon selects the optimal division point under fluorescence imaging. Further dissection of lymph nodes and fatty tissues is performed under white light, followed by low ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, preserving the left colic branch.
Under near-infrared fluorescence imaging, the surgeon confirms the blood flow in the preserved left colic artery, which appears illuminated by the fluorescence.
